title: Step by Step
developmental_frame: At 9–10, children often freeze at a task that looks 'too hard' because they see it as one big scary lump. Learning to break a tricky task into small steps and do them one at a time is a genuine superpower they can grasp now, especially with concrete tasks (a recipe, a build, a puzzle). Having learned to notice (Q1) and decide (Q2), they now learn to actually *tackle* something hard.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is getting through something that felt too hard by breaking it down — the proud realisation that 'hard' usually just means 'lots of small steps I haven't sorted yet'. It's the first taste of not giving up when something looks big.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child picks something they've wanted to do but that felt too hard, and conquers it by breaking it into steps. The triumph of finishing a 'too hard' thing — and realising the trick that made it possible — is powerfully motivating, and it builds the grit to try the next hard thing.

artifact: A tricky task the child broke into small steps and completed, with the steps written out as a 'step ladder' they followed.
success_criteria: The child took something that felt too big and broke it into small steps.
They put the steps in a sensible order and did them one at a time.
They finished the task, or got much further than they would have by charging in.

lessons.title: Big things feel scary in one lump
lessons.concept: When something looks 'too hard', it's usually because you're seeing the whole thing at once. 'Tidy my whole messy room' feels impossible. But it's really just a pile of small, easy jobs hiding inside one big scary word.
lessons.real_example: 'Bake a cake' sounds hard. But it's really: get ingredients, mix, pour, bake, wait, decorate. Six small steps — each one easy. The lump was the only scary part.
lessons.apply_task: Pick one real thing you've been putting off because it feels too big. Just name it for now.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Why has this felt too hard? Is it really hard, or just big?
lessons.feedback_task: Tell a parent your 'too big' task and see if they agree it's been feeling scary as one lump.
lessons.teach_back_task: Ask a family member about a task *they* find too big, and guess why it feels that way.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Pick something from your own real life — you know best what's been nagging at you. This is your task to name.
success_criteria: One real 'too big' task is named.

lessons.title: Break it into a step ladder
lessons.concept: Now chop the big task into small steps — each one a single easy thing you could do in a few minutes. Write them as a ladder, bottom to top. Suddenly the impossible thing is just a list of easy things.
lessons.real_example: 'Tidy my room' becomes: 1) pick up clothes, 2) put books on shelf, 3) clear the desk, 4) bin the rubbish, 5) make the bed. Five easy rungs instead of one scary word.
lessons.apply_task: Break your task into small steps and write them as a step ladder, in order.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Does the task look less scary now it's a list of small steps? That shrinking feeling is the whole trick.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your step ladder to someone. Ask if any step is still too big and needs splitting again.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a sibling turn one of their big tasks into a step ladder.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Try breaking it down yourself first — figuring out the steps is the brain exercise. If you get stuck, an adult can help more than a screen, because they know your task.
success_criteria: The task is broken into ordered small steps.

lessons.title: One step at a time
lessons.concept: Now just do the first step. Then the next. Don't look at the whole ladder — look at the one rung in front of you. Ticking off steps one by one is how big things actually get done.
lessons.real_example: You don't climb a ladder by jumping to the top — you take one rung at a time. Same with your task: first step done, tick, next step. Before long, you're at the top.
lessons.apply_task: Do your task one step at a time, ticking off each step as you finish it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to tick off each step? Small wins add up to a big finish.
lessons.feedback_task: Show someone your ticked-off ladder as you go. Let them cheer each step.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why doing 'one rung at a time' beats staring at the whole ladder.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is you doing the real work, step by step. The doing is yours — a computer can't tick your rungs for you.
success_criteria: The child worked through the steps in order, tracking progress.

lessons.title: Stuck? Try, tweak, ask
lessons.concept: Sometimes a step is harder than expected. That's normal — it doesn't mean stop. Try it, tweak your way if it doesn't work, and ask for help if you're really stuck. Getting unstuck is part of the skill.
lessons.real_example: If 'put books on the shelf' fails because the shelf is full, you tweak: maybe some books go in a box. A blocked step isn't the end — it's a little puzzle inside the big one.
lessons.apply_task: Find the step that was trickiest and write what you did to get past it — a tweak, a try, or asking someone.
lessons.reflection_prompt: When you got stuck, what helped most — trying again, changing your plan, or asking? Remember it for next time.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell an adult about a step you got stuck on and how you got unstuck. Ask if they'd have done it differently.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone the 'try, tweak, ask' trick for when a step gets hard.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: When stuck, a real person who knows your task usually helps more than a screen. Learn to ask for help — that's a strength, not a weakness.
success_criteria: The task was finished or substantially advanced.
The child can describe how they handled a stuck moment.
